perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to its conflicts of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the People's Republic of China, without regard to its conflicts of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from Delaware to the People's Republic of China may create inconsistencies if the agreement involves U.S. entities or activities that are subject to U.S. laws, such as export controls or intellectual property rights. The PRC's laws on trade secrets, for example, differ significantly from those in the US and could be detrimental to one of the parties.
perturbation.law_explanation: If the agreement involves the export of goods, software, or technology subject to the EAR, applying PRC law could hinder enforcement of U.S. export controls and create conflicts of law regarding compliance obligations and jurisdiction.

perturbation.justification: The change of governing law from Delaware to the People's Republic of China introduces a contradiction. The Law Explanation states that applying PRC law could hinder enforcement of U.S. export controls if the agreement involves the export of goods, software, or technology subject to the EAR. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines what is 'Subject to the EAR', implying that if the EAR applies, US law should govern. Changing the governing law to PRC law when EAR might apply creates a potential contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Term. This Agreement shall commence on the Effective Date and shall continue for a period of three (3) years thereafter, unless earlier terminated as provided herein.
perturbation.changed_text: Term. This Agreement shall commence on the Effective Date and shall continue for a period of ten (10) years thereafter, unless earlier terminated as provided herein.
perturbation.explanation: While a 10-year term isn't inherently illegal, some states have laws that limit the enforceability of non-compete agreements or confidentiality agreements beyond a certain period, if such clauses exist within the agreement or in conjunction with it.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits non-compete agreements. A ten-year confidentiality term might be deemed an unlawful restraint of trade if it effectively prevents a party from engaging in their profession or business.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the agreement term from three to ten years. The Law Explanation suggests this could violate California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which prohibits non-compete agreements if the extended term effectively prevents someone from engaging in their profession. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." Therefore, if the ten-year term in conjunction with confidentiality or non-compete clauses (as indicated in the Perturbation Explanation) unreasonably restrains trade, it directly contradicts the law provided.

perturbation.original_text: IN WITNESS WHEREOF, the parties have executed this Agreement as of the Effective Date.
perturbation.changed_text: IN WITNESS WHEREOF, the parties have executed this Agreement as of January 1, 1970.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the effective date to January 1, 1970, could create legal issues related to statutes of limitations, especially if the agreement involves intellectual property or confidential information that has since become public knowledge. This could also cause conflicts if one of the parties was not even established in 1970.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Statute of Limitations for Breach of Contract (Varies by State)
perturbation.law_explanation: In Delaware (as selected in a prior perturbation), the statute of limitations for breach of contract is three years. Setting the effective date to 1970 makes it impossible to bring a claim under this contract due to the expiration of the statute of limitations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: § 8106 Actions subject to 3-year limitation. :: 2015 Delaware Code :: US Codes and Statutes :: US Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the Delaware Code 2024 Delaware Code 2023 Delaware Code 2022 Delaware Code 2015 Delaware Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2015 Delaware Code Title 10 - Courts and Judicial Procedures CHAPTER 81. PERSONAL ACTIONS § 8106 Actions subject to 3-year limitation. 10 DE Code § 8106 (2015) What's This? (a) No action to recover damages for trespass, no action to regain possession of personal chattels, no action to recover damages for the detention of personal chattels, no action to recover a debt not evidenced by a record or by an instrument under seal, no action based on a detailed statement of the mutual demands in the nature of debit and credit between parties arising out of contractual or fiduciary relations, no action based on a promise, no action based on a statute, and no action to recover damages caused by an injury unaccompanied with force or resulting indirectly from the act of the defendant shall be brought after the expiration of 3 years from the accruing of the cause of such action; subject, however, to the provisions of §§ 8108-8110, 8119 and 8127 of this title. (b) Contractual limitations. — Notwithstanding any other provision of this section, a property insurance contract subject to subchapter III of Chapter 41 of Title 18: (1) May not require that an action for a claim made under the contract be filed less than 1 year from the date of the denial of the claim by the insurer; and (2) May permit an action for a claim made under the contract to be filed more than 1 year from the date of the denial of the claim by the insurer. (c) Notwithstanding anything to the contrary in this chapter (other than subsection (b) of this section) or in § 2-725 of Title 6, an action based on a written contract, agreement or undertaking involving at least $100,000 may be brought within a period specified in such written contr
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the effective date to January 1, 1970. The Law Explanation states that in Delaware, the statute of limitations for breach of contract is three years. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this could cause issues related to statutes of limitations. Scraped Law Snippet 2, § 8106, specifies a 3-year limitation for actions based on a promise or statute. By setting the effective date to 1970, any claim under the contract would be time-barred, creating a contradiction with the legal requirement that actions can be brought within the statute of limitations. The modified contract directly undermines the possibility of legal recourse within the legally prescribed timeframe, therefore, a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Injunctive Relief. Each party acknowledges that any breach of this Agreement may cause irreparable harm to the other party for which monetary damages would be inadequate, and the non-breaching party shall be entitled to seek injunctive relief, in addition to any other remedies available at law or in equity.
perturbation.changed_text: Injunctive Relief. Each party acknowledges that any breach of this Agreement may cause irreparable harm to the other party for which monetary damages would be inadequate, and the non-breaching party shall be entitled to seek injunctive relief, however such relief is limited to $100.00.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting injunctive relief to a specific monetary amount ($100.00) effectively nullifies the purpose of injunctive relief, which is to prevent ongoing or future harm that cannot be adequately compensated by money. This contradicts the fundamental principles of equity and the availability of injunctive relief where monetary damages are insufficient.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of Equity and Injunctive Relief
perturbation.law_citation: Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 65 outlines the procedure for obtaining a preliminary injunction or temporary restraining order. It does not specify dollar limits. The key point is that injunctive relief is for preventing irreparable harm, not for paying a fixed sum. This change limits the availability of injunctive relief, which is contrary to the spirit and purpose of equitable remedies.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders (a) Preliminary Injunction. (1) Notice. The court may issue a preliminary injunction only on notice to the adverse party. (2) Consolidating the Hearing with the Trial on the Merits. Before or after beginning the hearing on a motion for a preliminary injunction, the court may advance the trial on the merits and consolidate it with the hearing. Even when consolidation is not ordered, evidence that is received on the motion and that would be admissible at trial becomes part of the trial record and need not be repeated at trial. But the court must preserve any party's right to a jury trial. (b) Temporary Restraining Order. (1) Issuing Without Notice. The court may issue a temporary restraining order without written or oral notice to the adverse party or its attorney only if: (A) specific facts in an affidavit or a verified complaint clearly show that immediate and irreparable injury, loss, or damage will result to the movant before the adverse party can be heard in opposition; and (B) the movant's attorney certifies in writing any efforts made to give notice and the reasons why it should not be required. (2) Contents; Expiration. Every temporary restraining order issued without notice must state the date and hour it was issued; describe the injury and state why it is irreparable; state why the order was issued without notice; and be promptly filed in the clerk's office and entered in the record. The order expires at the time after entry—not to exceed 14 days—that the court sets, unless before that time the court, for good cause, extends it for a like period or the adverse party consents to a longer extension. The reasons for an extension must be entered in the record. (3) Expediting the Preliminary-Injunction Hearing. If the order is issued without notice, the moti

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a monetary limit to injunctive relief, directly contradicting its fundamental purpose, which is to prevent irreparable harm not adequately addressable by monetary damages. The original contract allowed seeking injunctive relief without limitation. The changed text caps this relief, undermining its effectiveness. Although the scraped snippets do not explicitly forbid capping injunctive relief, the Law Explanation states that injunctive relief is for preventing irreparable harm, not paying a fixed sum, and this change limits its availability. This aligns with the Perturbation Explanation that capping the relief nullifies its purpose. Rule 65 addresses injunctions and restraining orders but doesn't speak to the possibility of capping the amount.

perturbation.original_text: Entire Agreement. This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes all prior or contemporaneous communications and proposals, whether oral or written.
perturbation.changed_text: Entire Agreement. This Agreement constitutes a partial agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and does not supersede all prior or contemporaneous communications and proposals, whether oral or written.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'entire agreement' to 'partial agreement' and stating it does not supersede prior communications creates uncertainty about the terms of the agreement. It could allow prior oral agreements or understandings to be introduced, potentially contradicting the parol evidence rule. This is applicable in most jurisdictions.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-202, as adopted by Delaware, generally prevents the introduction of extrinsic evidence of prior or contemporaneous agreements to contradict a fully integrated written contract. By stating the agreement is 'partial' and does not supersede prior communications, the contract opens the door to such extrinsic evidence, undermining the certainty the parol evidence rule is designed to provide. Note that this applies even if the agreement doesn't specifically involve the sale of goods, as courts often apply the UCC by analogy.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-202. Final Written Expression: Parol or Extrinsic Evidence. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-202. Final Written Expression: Parol or Extrinsic Evidence. Terms with respect to which the confirmatory memoranda of the parties agree or which are otherwise set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to such terms as are included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of any prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement but may be explained or supplemented (a) by course of dealing or usage of trade (Section 1-205 ) or by course of performance (Section 2-208 ); and (b) by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the court finds the writing to have been intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement . Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the 'entire agreement' clause to a 'partial agreement' and states that it does not supersede prior communications. This directly contradicts the parol evidence rule, as articulated in UCC § 2-202. The scraped law snippet states that the terms of a final written agreement 'may not be contradicted by evidence of any prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement'. By explicitly stating the agreement is 'partial' and doesn't supersede prior communications, the modified contract opens the door for such contradictions. This undermines the purpose of having a written agreement, making the change a significant legal contradiction.
